did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning architectures, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) such as DenseNet121, ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and Vision Transformers (ViT), to perform binary classification of melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopic images. Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM were also employed for model interpretability.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results from a benchmarking study of eleven deep learning architectures (CNNs and Vision Transformers) for melanoma classification. It details a specific methodology using the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset, reports new performance metrics (e.g., 93.70% accuracy for DenseNet121), and includes a confusion matrix and Grad-CAM visualizations. Although published in a conference proceedings (ICCIT), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Classification Using CNNs and Vision Transformers with Explainable AI
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study benchmarks eleven deep learning architectures, including CNNs and Vision Transformers, for the binary classification of melanoma skin lesions using dermoscopic images from the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset. The models were trained with transfer learning using ImageNet weights, advanced preprocessing, and data augmentation to enhance robustness. Among the evaluated architectures, the proposed DenseNet121 model achieved the highest test accuracy of 93.70%, outperforming baselines such as ResNet50 and ViT variants. Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, were employed to visualize discriminative regions and improve model interpretability for clinical trust. The results demonstrate that optimized deep learning pipelines can provide robust and explainable support tools for early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two binary classes in the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset used for classification. The dataset contained 4,605 malignant training images and 500 malignant testing images. The study focused on detecting malignancy indicators such as asymmetry and irregular patterns.

One of the two binary classes in the Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset used for classification. The dataset contained 5,000 benign training images and 500 benign testing images. These served as the negative class for the binary melanoma classification task.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of melanoma skin lesions into benign and malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification of dermoscopic images into two discrete diagnostic categories: benign (label 0) and malignant (label 1). The study benchmarks eleven deep learning architectures (CNNs and Vision Transformers) for this task, reporting metrics such as accuracy (93.70% for DenseNet121), precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC. The target is a discrete class label, not a continuous value.

datasets_used.name: Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset from Kaggle consisting of clinically labeled dermoscopic images. Labels were derived from clinical diagnoses without histopathological verification. Images are in RGB JPEG format, resized to 224x224 pixels.

use.training: Used for training the deep learning models (CNNs and Vision Transformers) with transfer learning from ImageNet weights.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the final performance of the models, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score without statistical significance testing.

Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset.notes: DenseNet121 achieved the highest accuracy among all evaluated models. The model demonstrated strong class separability in PCA and t-SNE visualizations. Grad-CAM visualizations indicated that malignant cases showed significant activations in regions with asymmetry and irregular patterns, while benign cases showed little activity surrounding the tumor.

architecture_used.main_model: DenseNet121 (Primary), with comparative evaluation of ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, MobileNetV2, InceptionV3, Xception, VGG16, VGG19, CustomCNN, ViT-B16, and ViT-B32

architecture_details.transition_layers: Transition Layer 1 (28, 28, 128), Transition Layer 2 (14, 14, 256), Transition Layer 3 (7, 7, 512)
architecture_details.custom_cnn_layers: Conv3x3, BatchNorm, ReLU, Dropout, Fully Connected (FC), Softmax

training_details.loss_function: Sparse Categorical Crossentropy (for CNNs), Binary Crossentropy (for ViT)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels (384x384 for ViT-B32)
Normalization of pixel values to [0, 1] range by dividing by 255
Data augmentation: random horizontal and vertical flips, rotations up to ±90 degrees, brightness adjustments within ±20% range, random zooming up to 20%
Quality control: detection and removal of corrupted or duplicate files

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied on-the-fly using TensorFlow's preprocessing layers to simulate real-world variations in lesion orientation.
Applied up to ±90 degrees to enhance model generalization regarding lesion orientation.
Applied within a ±20% range to simulate variations in lighting conditions.
Applied up to 20% of the original image size to simulate variations in scale.
Used to handle class imbalance in the dataset by optimizing weighted loss functions with the Adam optimizer.

conclusions: The DenseNet121 architecture achieved the highest test accuracy of 93.70% for binary melanoma classification, outperforming other evaluated models including Vision Transformers (ViT-B16: 92.90%, ViT-B32: 91.50%), InceptionV3 (91.60%), Xception (91.50%), MobileNetV2 (91.20%), ResNet50 (88.40%), and EfficientNetB0 (82.50%).
The proposed DenseNet121 model demonstrated a high Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.97, indicating a reliable balance of sensitivity and specificity across thresholds.

Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, successfully visualized discriminative regions, showing significant activations in areas with asymmetry and irregular patterns for malignant cases and little activity for benign cases, thereby enhancing clinical trust and model interpretability.

The study acknowledges limitations regarding the dataset's lack of histopathological verification and potential issues with generalizability across diverse skin tones and conditions due to the use of a single public dataset.

The authors note that many existing top models are computationally expensive, hindering real-time clinical use, and suggest future work should focus on increasing model efficiency through quantization and pruning for deployment on edge devices.
important_remarks: The dataset labels were derived from clinical diagnoses without histopathological verification, which limits the ground truth reliability.
The study relies on a single public dataset (Kaggle Melanoma Skin Cancer Dataset), raising concerns about generalizability across diverse skin tones, demographics, and imaging conditions.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the provided test split of the Kaggle dataset.
The paper did not report any statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for performance comparisons between models.
The dataset exhibits mild class imbalance (4,605 malignant vs. 5,000 benign training images), though class weighting was used to address this.
The study acknowledges that many top-performing models are computationally expensive, hindering real-time clinical deployment, and suggests future work on quantization and pruning for edge devices.
Explainable AI (Grad-CAM) was used for interpretability, but the authors note that XAI methods often lack full clinical interpretability and further strategies are needed for clinical adoption.
